items.title:
Getting lots of questions on why the landing page / docs were updated if only 2% of new signups were affected.

This was understandably confusing for the 98% of folks not part of the experiment, and w
items.snippet:
Getting lots of questions on why the landing page / docs were updated if only 2% of new signups were affected.

This was understandably confusing for the 98% of folks not part of the experiment, and we've reverted both the landing page and docs changes.

items.title: For clarity, we're running a small test on ~2% of new prosumer signups. Existing Pro and Max subscribers aren't affected.
items.snippet: For clarity, we're running a small test on ~2% of new prosumer signups. Existing Pro and Max subscribers aren't affected.

items.title:
Opus 4.7 is a much more thorough and precise model. 

It does use more thinking tokens, and our new tokenizer can also create more tokens (up to 1.35x for the same input content).

To offset this, we'
items.snippet:
Opus 4.7 is a much more thorough and precise model. 

It does use more thinking tokens, and our new tokenizer can also create more tokens (up to 1.35x for the same input content).

To offset this, we've increased Opus 4.7 rate limits accordingly on our subscription plans. Enjoy!

items.title:
Opus 4.7 is out! Live on our API, Claude Code, Cowork, and Claude chat.

Thing I'm noticing internally: people are re-scoping what they hand to the model. Work that got chunked into small pieces for 4
items.snippet:
Opus 4.7 is out! Live on our API, Claude Code, Cowork, and Claude chat.

Thing I'm noticing internally: people are re-scoping what they hand to the model. Work that got chunked into small pieces for 4.6 because it was too ambiguous or too long is now going in as one task.

items.title:
Introducing Claude Opus 4.7, our most capable Opus model yet.

It handles long-running tasks with more rigor, follows instructions more precisely, and verifies its own outputs before reporting back.
items.snippet:
Introducing Claude Opus 4.7, our most capable Opus model yet.

It handles long-running tasks with more rigor, follows instructions more precisely, and verifies its own outputs before reporting back.

You can hand off your hardest work with less supervision. https://t.co/PtlRdpQcG5

items.title:
New research result: we use Claude to make fully autonomous progress on scalable oversight research, as measured by performance gap recovered (PGR).

Claude iterates on a number of different technique
items.snippet:
New research result: we use Claude to make fully autonomous progress on scalable oversight research, as measured by performance gap recovered (PGR).

Claude iterates on a number of different techniques and ends up significantly outperforming human researchers for $18k in credits. https://t.co/fbVpCP

items.title:

items.title:
Now in research preview: routines in Claude Code.

Configure a routine once (a prompt, a repo, and your connectors), and it can run on a schedule, from an API call, or in response to an event.

Routin
items.snippet:
Now in research preview: routines in Claude Code.

Configure a routine once (a prompt, a repo, and your connectors), and it can run on a schedule, from an API call, or in response to an event.

Routines run on our web infrastructure, so you don't have to keep your laptop open. https://t.co/m2XJWYqkf

items.title:
In March we published a paper on alignment audits: teams of humans were tasked to find the problems in model we trained to be misaligned.

Now we have agents that can do it automatically 42% of the ti
items.snippet:
In March we published a paper on alignment audits: teams of humans were tasked to find the problems in model we trained to be misaligned.

Now we have agents that can do it automatically 42% of the time.

items.title:
If you don't train your CoTs to look nice, you could get some safety from monitoring them.

This seems good to do!

But I'm skeptical this will work reliably enough to be load-bearing in a safety case
items.snippet:
If you don't train your CoTs to look nice, you could get some safety from monitoring them.

This seems good to do!

But I'm skeptical this will work reliably enough to be load-bearing in a safety case.

Plus as RL is scaled up, I expect CoTs to become less and less legible.

items.title: So many things to love about Claude 4! My favorite is that the model is so strong that we had to turn on additional safety mitigations according to Anthropic's responsible scaling policy
